mnemonics: K-NN: K-Nearest Neighbors. Think of it as 'K' is for 'Kindred' – finding your closest kindred spirits in the data to make a prediction.
Instance-Based Learning: Remember 'I B.L.' as 'I Be-Long?' The new instance asks the stored examples, 'Who do I belong with?'
keyPoints: Lazy Learning: Methods like K-NN do not build an explicit model during training; all computation is deferred until prediction time.
Similarity is Key: Predictions are made based on the similarity (e.g., distance metrics like Euclidean) between a new instance and stored training examples.
The 'K' in K-NN is a hyperparameter that controls the bias-variance trade-off; a small K leads to high variance (overfitting), a large K leads to high bias (underfitting).
commonMistakes: Forgetting to normalize/scale features when using distance-based metrics, which causes features on larger scales to disproportionately dominate the distance calculation.
Treating K-NN as a parametric model that builds an explicit function during training, when it is actually a non-parametric, instance-based method.
